speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: Penina, you're surrounded by your beautiful family. Can you introduce us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. This is Harold Bowman, my husband, Harold. And we have three children. Our oldest is Leora her last name now is Wollner, and this is her husband, Herb Wollner. And their two sons are Matthew Wollner and Robert. And our next child was Allan Bowman and behind me is Deborah Bowman-Leafman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that any of you would like to say to your mother about her experiences, or about her as a person? [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could say that I always admired her positive attitude. How she was always able to look to the future and live life to the fullest, and not dwell on the past and all the hardships that she had and the rest of her family. You know, her message always was get involved and do things and-- and be positive. She's spoken to the kids at the school I teach at several times, and it-- it really made an impact on them also, I think, to actually hear her stories and to meet somebody who lived through the horrors she did, but yet, could be so positive and optimistic and-- and happy about life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: You'd be next, age-wise, but if you want me to go, I'll go. I remember as a child being very scared to hear anything about her experiences. It was a little bit too real, and I didn't know what to do with her experiences. And as an adult, as I got older, I think now I've come to, as Leora just said, admire her very much. And still-- I still feel a little bit like I'm in awe when I hear these stories. It-- it's still-- I still can't really believe that this happened to my mother, along with everyone else that it happened to. I'm currently-- I just have to say this part-- I'm currently in a production of-- or will be soon, when it opens-- of And one reason I really, really wanted to do this show is, there was something about kind of coming full circle with how I understand my mother's experiences. And I'm kind of doing this show for her. And it's, I think, going to be hard for me to do it. It's hard for me to even talk about it right now, but it's-- it's a real tribute to my mother to be able to do this show. I'm very proud of her and proud to be able to do that for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Sweet.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think when we were children, we didn't hear too many of the stories. It wasn't really talked about a lot in the house until we were older and moving out. And what I remember most is when I graduated college, and we went to Europe and saw some of these places and saw her hometown. And we visited Salzburg, and we went to Dachau, that kind of made things hit home, and made me really bring everything together and understand what was going on more. And we talked about it some on that trip. And since then, we've heard more of the stories, and you know, we-- now we understand better. But growing up as kids, I think, we didn't get the full picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the young man? Would you like to say something to your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I appreciate how she came to our-- my high school and talked to my whole class, and let them know more about it than just how our teacher felt about it, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember in about third grade when I went to the Epstein School, she would come-- she came one day, and she-- she was talking about her experiences. And I remember how every day since, if I ever talked about my grandmother, they would-- they would always remember. Oh, she's the one that came to talk to us about the school. And they could remember some of the stuff that I just was amazed that they remembered about her, just from that one day that she spoke. And I was a little surprised that they-- considering that we were, like, in third grade, that they still remembered stuff about her from back then. And I was pretty happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have all the letters. You want to check them out? I have a folder with them. I must have a couple hundred letters from every grade that I spoke at that school. That's my pay. I want them to write to me. And I have them filed away, according to the grades. And that was the last speech I gave was in April of this year, and I-- I think your grandchild's name is on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Harold, can you say something about perhaps how your experiences, and your life with Penina, have affected how y'all have brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe I'm not being directly responsive, but I think that the most significant thing that comes to my mind as I look back is the tremendous growth of Penny as a person, self-educated almost completely with very minimal preparation beforehand and just striving all the time to reach out for other educational and social experiences, until she becomes the person she is today, so active in so many things, and involved, and interested. And I think that it's just a huge leap that she has made, and she's done it basically all on her own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anybody else like to add anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have met quite a few people who are Holocaust survivors through the years, and one thing that always struck me was how quiet and introspective they were, and how they really didn't get involved that much in community or other things. But Penny has always been outgoing. Her bright, cheerful nature is certainly something that I don't see in many other Holocaust survivors. And her attitude has prevailed throughout the family, and it's a good, positive attitude. And I think it's wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. Thank you so much, all of you. Oh, one minute-- one other thing. Penny-- Penina, if you had a message to give to your children, and your grandchildren, and great-grandchildren to come one day, what would that message be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always like to tell my-- my-- the people I speak to-- and the same thing, message could hold true for my immediate family-- is not to hate. Not to hate because-- to hate a system or an event, but not the person and to become involved. And to stop the smallest thing happening before it be-- grows-- grows and becomes big. And to carry on the legacy of the Holocaust, and-- and not to let it die out. Just like the Jewish history, Passover-- throughout every year, to remember what happened in order to prevent it from happening again. That's the only way we will prevent the Holocaust-- by studying, learning the past. And not to forget, so we won't repeat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the only picture I have of my early childhood. I was about 14 years old there, sitting in a park with my sister Yaffa, reading, which was our favorite pastime. I think I even remember reading Will Durant book there. It's a fat book. And the reason I have this picture is because my sister gave her photo album to a next door neighbor, a Gentile neighbor, and they guarded the book. And we-- we survived and went back to get the photo album. We had this picture, and it was a real tiny picture, which I had enlarged. It was taken in Kalusz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my three uncles that survived the war. My father had seven-- there were seven brothers, and these are the three that survived the war. And the one Uncle Josef, Uncle Aryeh and Shmuel, and the one on the right with the beard looks very similar to my father. And the picture above it is my-- my-- their parents, my grandparents, that was preserved.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the uncles had found the picture in-- in Palestine. That's why we have it. People who immigrated in-- in '38, before the war, and they had the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Pierre Harvard, who was the Frenchman who worked in the electronics factory and gave me packages. And after the war, he wanted me to go with him and gave me the ring. But I didn't want to go with him. But he gave me several pictures, and this is one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my tiny little diary that I made when I worked in the electronics factory from pages that Pierre gave me, put together with lead. It has the little lead button with my number on it. And I treasured it so much all the years. And because it was so tiny, I kept it in my jewelry box. And one day, our house was broken in in Houston, and they took all my jewelry. And this little diary was in that box, in the jewelry case. So everything was taken, and I don't have it. Luckily, I had it-- I had translated what it said, and I have a picture of my writing in it. But the diary I don't have anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the inside page of the diary. It-- it has a date of April 19, 1945, in honor of my 18th birthday. And it's written in Hungarian. And I-- I-- I mentioned previously what I was writing about-- the hope that I have that people still nice, can do nice things to me. And then the other side has-- the cover shows 542, which was our prisoner number.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph taken by Harold during our courtship days in Salzburg, Austria, when I was in the displaced persons camp. And that was the barracks in back of us, the headquarters that we lived in. And we-- we-- we loved playing in the snow, and that's snow. And Harold brought me some extra clothes and a-- a blanket, and I made slacks out of it-- I think I'm not wearing slacks there-- but I-- to play in the snow. But this-- this was taken in-- right after the war in 1945, October '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Cyprus by British correspondents who were sent from around the world to take pictures and show the conditions of the Holocaust survivors who were waiting for deportation to-- to-- immigration to Palestine. And this was taken by a British photographer, which I received after the war. This shows our primitive living quarters. We-- we had just bare wood that we slept on, makeshift stoves, and canned goods that we used for dishes. And this shows getting wood, trying to burn the stove, and warm up food.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, all right. Go ahead. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this-- this is a picture also taken in Cyprus. And on the right there with-- just wearing straps-- bras, fancy clothes that we made from-- from handkerchiefs. We didn't have any clothes, and we made a few clothes. And there's a table. We're sitting outside, and we are all sewing. And the men are trying to make a lantern to use at night. And they're all people from my kibbutz. And the woman on the-- on the left was the one that helped me make my wedding dress. And that's me, [INAUDIBLE] Weisz, on the right. This was also taken by the British--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this-- this is one of the agencies that helped Harold and I get in touch with after the war, when I was travelling as a displaced person to Palestine, and we lost touch. And this is the Association of Settlers from Hungary. There were several organizations that helped us get in touch with each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the Chief Rabbi's Religious Emergency Council, and they were also trying to help the survivors find each other. And this was one-- one of the letters trying to tell Harold where I was. This was written in October '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the American Jewish Joint Distribution, who came to-- also to Cyprus, and try and find contacts between people. And we also received our clothes and packages from this organization. This was dated in August of '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my identity paper. When we arrived in Palestine, the British issued it, and it's the certificate of identity. And this was the first document that I had after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding dress. I got married in a kibbutz in Binyamin. And it's very special, because when we were dating, Harold used to bring me gifts in Salzburg. An American soldier would make all kinds of little gifts. And one day, he brought me the fabric, and I was determined to save it for when we got married. But when we left Salzburg and left Italy to go to Palestine, we weren't allowed to carry any extra packages with us-- just barely what we had on our backs. And so I took the fabric, and I sewed it into the lining-- as a lining in my coat. And finally in Palestine, when I got married in '47, a friend of mine and I made this dress, and we got married March 30, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's all handmade. A slip-- a matching slip under it. And a very short dress. I guess we were fashionable. Look how short it is. I treasure it even though I can't do anything with it.
